reasoning: Google Chrome's current market share is massive, estimated at around 65% across all devices, with Safari a distant second at around 20%. This lead has been stable for many years. The resolution criteria rely on the "Usage share of all browsers" table in the relevant Wikipedia article, which pulls from sources like StatCounter. StatCounter tracks Chrome as the dominant browser. There is practically no plausible scenario in which Safari or another browser eclipses Chrome's market share by the end of 2025 (a matter of 4.5 months). Even rapid adoption of a completely new browser (e.g., an AI-integrated browser or ChatGPT search) could not steal 45% of the global browser market share by the end of the year, given the inertia of default browsers on Android and enterprise/consumer habits. Furthermore, while the US DOJ is pursuing an antitrust breakup that could force Google to sell Chrome, these legal proceedings take years, and any potential sale would not be finalized or implemented by December 2025. Even if a sale miraculously occurred, the browser would almost certainly still be tracked as "Chrome" by Wikipedia/Statcounter due to continuity. The Manifold base rate for this question was an extremely confident 0.97 in early August. Due to the overwhelming evidence and essentially zero risk of sudden market shifts of this magnitude in such a short window, 0.98 is the appropriate and maximum permitted probability.
